worth_it_explanation: Account ownership and access are the first things that break when a person leaves or an agency engagement ends, and the cost of getting them wrong is high. An ad account tied to one personal login can lock the advertiser out of its own spend, its Matched Audiences, and its conversion history. The checks here are quick to run from the account's user list and the Business Manager view, and the fix is a configuration change rather than a rebuild. Confirming who owns the account and who holds which role takes a few minutes and prevents a recovery scramble later.
intro: A LinkedIn ad account can be run two ways. It can sit inside Business Manager, where an organization holds the ad accounts, the people, and the Matched Audiences centrally, or it can run from a personal Campaign Manager login where one person's account holds the access. How the account is owned decides what happens when that person leaves and how cleanly an agency can hand the account back. These checks confirm the ownership model and that each person holds a role matched to the work they do.

audit_checks.title: The account is run from a personal Campaign Manager rather than Business Manager
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When an ad account is tied to a single personal login rather than an organization, access leaves with the person. If that login is lost, disabled, or belongs to someone who departs, the advertiser can lose entry to its own spend, Matched Audiences, and conversion history, and recovery depends on LinkedIn support rather than a self-serve transfer.

educate.base:
LinkedIn offers two ways to hold an ad account. Business Manager is an organization-level container where a company owns its ad accounts, its people, and its Matched Audiences in one place, and access is granted to individuals from there. A personal Campaign Manager setup, by contrast, ties the ad account to one person's LinkedIn login, and that person's access is the account's access.

When an account runs from a personal login, the practical risk is continuity. If that person leaves the company, loses access to their LinkedIn account, or has the account restricted, the advertiser can be cut off from its own campaigns, billing, and the Matched Audiences it has built. Recovering an account in that state often means working through LinkedIn support rather than reassigning access in a self-serve panel.

Business Manager also makes agency handoffs cleaner. Because the organization owns the account, an agency can be granted access and later removed without the account itself changing hands, and the advertiser keeps everything that was built during the engagement.

A few signs point to a personal setup:
  • The ad account is not listed under any Business Manager
  • A single individual is the billing admin and the only account manager
  • Access was set up through a personal connection invite rather than a Business Manager invitation
You may want to confirm whether the account sits inside Business Manager and, if it does not, consider moving it under one so ownership rests with the organization rather than a person. LinkedIn documents how Business Manager roles and permissions work if you are weighing the move.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a considered-purchase or business-to-business (B2B) store advertising on LinkedIn, the account often holds remarketing and Matched Audiences built from site visitors and customer lists over many months. If that account runs from one personal login, those audiences are at risk of being stranded. You may want to confirm the account sits inside Business Manager so the audiences belong to the organization.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-capture advertiser, the ad account usually holds the Lead Gen Forms, the conversion setup, and the audiences that feed pipeline. Tying all of that to a personal login puts continuity at risk if the person leaves. You may want to confirm the account is owned through Business Manager rather than an individual.

learn_more.source_title: Add people to ad accounts in Business Manager

investigate.intro: Confirm whether the ad account is held inside a Business Manager and how its owning access is structured in Campaign Manager.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Sign in to Campaign Manager and open the account you are auditing.
Open the account's settings and find the user list, which shows everyone with access and the role each person holds.
Note whether a single person is the only account manager and the billing admin, which points to a personal setup rather than a shared organizational one.
Check whether the account appears in a Business Manager view. An account managed only through Campaign Manager with no Business Manager listing is being run from personal access.

linkedin_business_manager.label: LinkedIn Business Manager
steps: Open Business Manager for the organization, if one exists.
Open the 'Ad accounts' section and check whether the account you are auditing is listed there.
If the account is absent from Business Manager, ownership currently rests with an individual rather than the organization.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the ad account is held inside a Business Manager and who holds the account manager and billing admin roles. An account listed under a Business Manager, with access granted to the organization rather than a lone individual, passes. An account that runs from a single personal login with no Business Manager listing is a fail recorded with the name of the sole owner.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the account is listed in a Business Manager and who holds the account manager and billing admin roles to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s LinkedIn ad account is run from a personal Campaign Manager login rather than held inside Business Manager. With ownership tied to one individual, access to the account, its billing, and its Matched Audiences leaves with that person, and recovering a stranded account depends on LinkedIn support rather than a self-serve transfer. {{voice_recommend}} moving the ad account under a Business Manager so the organization owns it, then granting each person access from there. After the move, confirm the account appears in the Business Manager 'Ad accounts' list and that more than one person holds the account manager role for continuity.

audit_checks.title: Users hold broader roles than their work requires
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When everyone with access holds the broadest role, any single mistake or compromised login can change billing, restructure campaigns, or remove other users. Granting each person only the role their work needs limits how much damage a wrong click or a stolen credential can do.

educate.base:
LinkedIn ad accounts use graduated roles so that each person's access can match what they actually do. The roles range from full control down to read-only, and each one grants a different set of actions.

The ad account roles break down by what each can do:
  • Billing admin: full control, including billing details and managing who has access
  • Account manager: campaigns, ads, and billing functions short of changing billing details
  • Campaign manager: create and edit campaigns and ads, without billing or user management
  • Creative manager: edit ads only
  • Viewer: read-only access to performance and billing history
When everyone is granted account manager or billing admin regardless of their role, the account widens the blast radius of any error. A contractor who only needs to read reports does not need to be able to change billing or remove other users, and a credential that is phished carries whatever powers the role gave it.

You may want to review the user list and consider granting each person the least role that lets them do their work. LinkedIn describes what each Campaign Manager role can do, which is a useful reference when right-sizing access. It is also worth confirming that at least two people hold the account manager role so the account is not dependent on one person, while keeping the broadest roles limited to those who need them.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A considered-purchase or business-to-business (B2B) store often shares its ad account with an agency and internal staff at the same time. You may want to confirm that agency users hold a campaign or creative role rather than billing admin, so external partners cannot change billing or remove the advertiser's own people.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead-capture advertiser frequently grants access to a sales operations or marketing contractor who only needs to read results. You may want to confirm those users hold the viewer role rather than a role that can edit campaigns or touch billing.

learn_more.source_title: User roles and permissions in Campaign Manager

investigate.intro: Review the account's user list and compare each person's role against the work they actually do.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Sign in to Campaign Manager and open the account you are auditing.
Open the account's settings and find the user list, which shows each person with access and their assigned role.
For each person, note their role and what their work requires, and flag anyone holding billing admin or account manager who only needs to view reports or edit creatives.
Confirm that at least two people hold the account manager role, so a single departure does not leave the account without a manager.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per user, the role held and the role the person's work requires. A user list where each person holds the least role their work needs, and where at least two people hold the account manager role, passes. Any user holding a broader role than their work requires, such as a report-only contractor set as billing admin, is a fail recorded with the person's name and the role mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user's assigned role and the role their work requires to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several users on {{site_name}}'s LinkedIn ad account hold broader roles than their work requires. When access is wider than the task, any single mistake or compromised login can change billing, restructure campaigns, or remove other users, so the wider the roles, the larger the damage a wrong click or a stolen credential can do. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the user list and granting each person the least role that lets them do their work, reserving billing admin and account manager for those who need them. After the change, confirm that report-only and creative users hold the viewer or creative manager role and that at least two people retain the account manager role for continuity.
